metadata.purpose: Dedicated evaluation dataset for sandbox technologies and agent sandbox integrations used to run AI-generated or agent-written code.
metadata.score_scale: 0-5, where 5 is strongest for the named criterion.

criteria.isolation_strength: Strength of the boundary between untrusted workload and host or other tenants.
criteria.workspace_reset: Ability to create clean, disposable, reproducible workspaces for each task.
criteria.network_control: Ability to default-deny, allowlist, audit, or otherwise constrain outbound and private-network access.
criteria.secret_boundary: Ability to keep host, CI, cloud, and application credentials outside the workload by default.
criteria.filesystem_mount_control: Ability to constrain mounts, writable paths, symlink behavior, and host filesystem exposure.
criteria.dependency_support: Ability to install packages, run builds, use real language tooling, and execute test suites.
criteria.agent_sdk_fit: Fit for AI coding-agent workflows, including programmatic creation, file APIs, command execution, and repo checkout.
criteria.observability: Quality of logs, command transcripts, metrics, artifacts, and failure debugging surfaces.
criteria.startup_speed: Expected speed to create a fresh environment for a task.
criteria.horizontal_scale: Fit for many parallel sandboxes, evaluations, or autonomous task queues.
criteria.self_hosting_control: Ability to self-host or run without depending on a proprietary managed service.
criteria.operational_ease: Ease of setup, upgrades, policy management, image management, and day-2 operations.
criteria.cost_predictability: Ability to budget runtime, storage, network, image, and queue cost.
criteria.maturity: Production maturity, documentation depth, ecosystem support, and maintenance signal.

local_developer_agents.label: Local developer agents
local_developer_agents.question: What sandbox model should protect local AI coding agents without making normal development painful?

autonomous_pr_security.label: Autonomous PR security
autonomous_pr_security.question: What sandbox model should run agent-written code that can open pull requests?

untrusted_user_code.label: Untrusted user code
untrusted_user_code.question: What sandbox model should run arbitrary user- or model-generated code from outside the organization?

evals_and_rl_scale.label: Evals and RL scale
evals_and_rl_scale.question: What sandbox model should run many repeatable code-execution trajectories?

enterprise_self_hosted.label: Enterprise self-hosted
enterprise_self_hosted.question: What sandbox model gives a security team maximum control over policy and runtime boundaries?

sandboxes.name: Docker rootless containers
sandboxes.license_or_terms: Apache-2.0 components and Docker project licensing; deployment terms depend on Docker products used.
sandboxes.summary: Rootless Docker runs the daemon and containers inside a user namespace so the daemon and container processes do not require host root privileges.
sandboxes.best_for: Local developer agents and CI-like workflows that need familiar container tooling.
sandboxes.watch_for: Container escapes remain a kernel/runtime risk; bind mounts, Docker socket exposure, privileged flags, and rootless-in-container workarounds can erase much of the benefit.

sandboxes.name: Podman rootless containers
sandboxes.summary: Podman provides daemonless, rootless container execution with Docker-compatible workflows and strong Linux integration.
sandboxes.best_for: Security-conscious local or enterprise Linux environments that want rootless containers without a long-running privileged daemon.
sandboxes.watch_for: Rootless networking, storage drivers, UID/GID mapping, and enterprise desktop support need deliberate setup.

sandboxes.name: gVisor runsc
sandboxes.summary: gVisor places a userspace application kernel between containers and the host kernel while preserving much of the container workflow.
sandboxes.best_for: Running untrusted containerized code when stronger isolation than normal containers is needed without moving fully to VMs.
sandboxes.watch_for: Syscall compatibility, filesystem performance, and debugging differences must be tested against real build/test workloads.

sandboxes.name: Firecracker microVMs
sandboxes.summary: Firecracker provides lightweight microVMs with hardware virtualization boundaries and a jailer process for additional host-side isolation.
sandboxes.best_for: High-assurance multi-tenant sandbox services where the team can own the control plane.
sandboxes.watch_for: It is a low-level building block: image lifecycle, networking, snapshots, storage, API service, and observability must be engineered.

sandboxes.name: Kata Containers
sandboxes.summary: Kata Containers integrates lightweight VM isolation into the container ecosystem, giving each container a VM-style boundary.
sandboxes.best_for: Enterprise Kubernetes or container-platform teams that want hardware virtualization as a second isolation layer.
sandboxes.watch_for: RuntimeClass, image, networking, Kubernetes integration, performance overhead, and VMM backend choices add operational work.

sandboxes.name: nsjail or bubblewrap process sandbox
sandboxes.license_or_terms: Apache-2.0 or LGPL-2.0-or-later depending on component.
sandboxes.summary: Linux namespace, cgroup, chroot, and seccomp tools can isolate a single process or controlled command without full container orchestration.
sandboxes.best_for: Narrow tools, parsers, compilers, or command-level tests where the allowed syscall and filesystem surface is small.
sandboxes.watch_for: Not a complete coding-agent environment by itself; policy must be built and validated carefully.

sandboxes.name: E2B sandboxes
sandboxes.license_or_terms: Apache-2.0 repository plus managed service terms.
sandboxes.summary: E2B provides isolated sandboxes for agents to execute code, process data, and run tools through SDKs.
sandboxes.best_for: Agent products that need a ready-made cloud sandbox API and do not want to operate the sandbox control plane.
sandboxes.watch_for: Managed-service dependency, data residency, cost, and secret-handling model must pass procurement and security review.

sandboxes.name: Daytona sandboxes
sandboxes.license_or_terms: Apache-2.0 repository plus managed service terms.
sandboxes.summary: Daytona targets fast, stateful, isolated infrastructure for AI-generated code with file, Git, LSP, and execution APIs.
sandboxes.best_for: Agent workflows that need a developer-like machine with programmatic file and command control.
sandboxes.watch_for: Validate actual isolation, tenancy, credential flow, and pricing under sustained task volume.

sandboxes.name: Modal Sandboxes
sandboxes.summary: Modal Sandboxes create isolated environments for untrusted code, repository checkout, arbitrary dependencies, and high-scale agent/eval workloads.
sandboxes.best_for: High-concurrency evaluations, RL rollouts, and production agent code execution where managed scale matters.
sandboxes.watch_for: Managed-service dependency and platform-specific observability/cost model must match the workload.

sandboxes.name: Vercel Sandbox
sandboxes.summary: Vercel Sandbox is a compute primitive for safely running untrusted or user-generated code for AI agents and dynamic workloads.
sandboxes.best_for: Web/app-generation agents already on Vercel that need ephemeral Linux sandboxes with real-time execution.
sandboxes.watch_for: Platform dependency, region/control-plane constraints, and whether the product's network/secret policy is sufficient for enterprise autonomy.

sandboxes.name: OpenHands runtime API sandbox
sandboxes.license_or_terms: MIT for SDK components; hosted runtime terms may apply.
sandboxes.summary: OpenHands SDK can connect agent servers to remote runtime APIs that provision sandboxed environments and manage container lifecycle.
sandboxes.best_for: Custom software-agent products that want an SDK-level remote workspace abstraction.
sandboxes.watch_for: Runtime service availability, image control, credential handling, and the exact container isolation policy must be verified.

sandboxes.name: Codex sandbox and approvals
sandboxes.license_or_terms: OpenAI product and CLI terms; open-source CLI components may differ by package.
sandboxes.summary: Codex combines sandbox modes and approval policies so agent tool calls operate within file and network boundaries and ask before crossing them.
sandboxes.best_for: Teams already using Codex that need local or CI agent execution with clear approval boundaries.
sandboxes.watch_for: It is an agent execution control, not a general-purpose untrusted-code platform; provider dependence and host-specific sandbox support matter.

sandboxes.name: Sandcastle provider sandboxes
sandboxes.summary: Sandcastle orchestrates coding agents in provider-backed sandboxes such as Docker, Podman, and Vercel with branch/worktree strategies.
sandboxes.best_for: TypeScript teams that want a coding-agent orchestration layer over explicit sandbox providers.
sandboxes.watch_for: Security depends on the chosen provider, image, mounts, branch merge policy, and network defaults.

sandboxes.name: Flue virtual sandbox
sandboxes.summary: Flue defaults to a lightweight in-memory virtual sandbox for agent workspaces and can be paired with stronger local or remote sandbox choices.
sandboxes.best_for: Document, repo-slice, or workflow tasks where the app can stage files and does not need arbitrary OS-level execution.
sandboxes.watch_for: The virtual sandbox is not equivalent to hard OS isolation for arbitrary untrusted code execution.

sandboxes.name: Kubernetes hardened pods
sandboxes.summary: Kubernetes security contexts, Pod Security Standards, seccomp, namespaces, admission policy, and network policy can create a governed sandbox layer.
sandboxes.best_for: Enterprise platform teams that already operate Kubernetes and need central policy, admission, and audit controls.
sandboxes.watch_for: A hardened pod is still container isolation unless combined with gVisor, Kata, or VM-backed runtime classes; policy drift is a real risk.

threats.threat: Agent-written code reads or modifies host files outside the intended workspace.
Generated code scans private services or exfiltrates data through unrestricted outbound network access.
Agent commands access API keys, CI secrets, cloud credentials, package tokens, or SSH material.
A malicious dependency or generated program exploits the runtime boundary.
Long-running agent loops consume sandbox minutes, tokens, storage, or queue slots.
